title: Font families and loading

publication.changeSummary: Technology-specific CSS: Responsive and Accessible Styling draft for Font families and loading; independent standards, browser, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CSS Snapshot 2024; W3C Working Group Note 2024; provisions applicable to Font families and loading
sources.claim: Font-family lists provide ordered fallbacks, web fonts need loading strategy, and font metrics affect wrapping, layout shift, and readability.

sources.locator: MDN CSS Guide; accessed 2026-07; provisions applicable to Font families and loading
sources.claim: Font-family lists provide ordered fallbacks, web fonts need loading strategy, and font metrics affect wrapping, layout shift, and readability.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Font-family lists provide ordered fallbacks, web fonts need loading strategy, and font metrics affect wrapping, layout shift, and readability.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: A system fallback renders immediately while the chosen web font loads with compatible metrics. Your goal is to configure a font stack and explain fallback, loading and privacy trade-offs. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Lengths, percentages and viewport units; html semantics text. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Technology rule
sections.body: Font-family lists provide ordered fallbacks, web fonts need loading strategy, and font metrics affect wrapping, layout shift, and readability. Apply this rule to the actual markup, stylesheet, JavaScript, or component state rather than memorizing its name.

sections.heading: Boundary and browser reality
sections.body: A downloaded font can fail, synthetic styles differ, variable axes need ranges, and icon fonts can expose incorrect text. This draft therefore rejects: Declaring one custom font guarantees it loads before any text is displayed.

includes: Define resilient font stacks and load web fonts without unreadable fallback assumptions.
Configure a font stack and explain fallback, loading and privacy trade-offs.
excludes: A downloaded font can fail, synthetic styles differ, variable axes need ranges, and icon fonts can expose incorrect text.

outcomes: Configure a font stack and explain fallback, loading and privacy trade-offs.

examples.title: Font families and loading: concrete case
examples.situation: A system fallback renders immediately while the chosen web font loads with compatible metrics.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the exact artifact and starting state: A system fallback renders immediately while the chosen web font loads with compatible metrics.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the correction: Provide appropriate fallback fonts and a measured font-display strategy because web-font loading can fail or arrive late.
examples.result: The result follows because Provide appropriate fallback fonts and a measured font-display strategy because web-font loading can fail or arrive late.

examples.title: Font families and loading: boundary case
examples.situation: A downloaded font can fail, synthetic styles differ, variable axes need ranges, and icon fonts can expose incorrect text.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Start from the documented boundary: A downloaded font can fail, synthetic styles differ, variable axes need ranges, and icon fonts can expose incorrect text.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Reject the misconception: Declaring one custom font guarantees it loads before any text is displayed.
examples.result: The boundary requires this correction: Provide appropriate fallback fonts and a measured font-display strategy because web-font loading can fail or arrive late.

analogy.example: A layered stage design can model Font families and loading, where rules size, place, and paint existing content.
usefulFor: Building a first mental model for Font families and loading before using exact platform terms
limitations: Physical layers do not reproduce cascade algorithms, intrinsic sizing, writing modes, or user preferences.
